### Key Takeaways

- Agents are systems not models – evaluate them accordingly. AI agents plan, call tools, maintain state, and adapt across multiple turns. Single-turn accuracy metrics and classical natural language processing (NLP) benchmarks like bilingual evaluation understudy (BLEU) and recall-oriented understudy for gisting evaluation (ROUGE) don't capture how agents fail in practice. Evaluation must target the full system's behavior over time.
- Behavior beats benchmarks. Task success, graceful recovery from tool failures, and consistency under real-world variability matter more than scoring well on curated test sets. An agent that works perfectly in a sandbox but silently misreports a failed refund in production hasn't passed any evaluation that counts.
- Hybrid evaluation is non-negotiable. Automated scoring (LLM-as-a-judge, trace analysis, and load testing) gives you repeatability and scale. Human judgment captures what automation misses: tone, trust, and contextual appropriateness. The best evaluation pipelines combine both, continuously.
- Operational constraints are first-class evaluation targets. Latency, cost per task, token efficiency, tool reliability, and policy compliance aren't afterthoughts, they are what determines whether a technically capable agent is viable at enterprise scale.
- Safety, governance, and user trust complete the picture. Red teaming, PII handling, permission boundary testing, and user experience scoring are as critical as accuracy. A technically brilliant agent that violates privacy boundaries or confuses users is a liability, not an asset.

## Introduction

You may have seen teams in your organization leveraging AI agents for demos, experiments, testing workflows where everything works perfectly. The agent plans, reasons, picks the right tool, and executes flawlessly during experiments. In production, the system fails or exhibits suboptimal behavior, and no one is quite sure whether the "smart" agent is actually reliable.

This article is for engineering and ML teams moving tool-using AI agents from prototype to production. It offers a practical evaluation framework, covering what to measure, how to measure it, and which tools to use, so you can catch failures before your users do.

The examples and code snippets in this article are intentionally minimal and illustrative, a one-sample Claude + LangChain evaluation meant to demonstrate both reference-free (helpfulness) and reference-aware (correctness) scoring, with a stable, versioned model for reproducibility. Production-grade evaluation pipelines require additional hardening around reliability, governance, cost control, version management, and data protection. In production setups, it is a good practice to use a separate judge model to reduce self-grading bias as shown in the code example.

In traditional software engineering, systems are rigorously tested before being deployed to production. AI agents, however, challenge this practice. While teams often validate individual models using established benchmarks, these evaluations rarely extend to the full agentic system operating in realistic environments. Unlike standard LLMs that generate single-turn text responses, AI agents are composite systems: They plan actions, invoke tools and APIs, maintain memory across interactions, and adapt their behavior over multiple steps and sessions. Classical NLP metrics like BLEU or ROUGE weren't designed for this situation; they score static text, not dynamic behavior. Consider a concrete example: An order-triage agent correctly identifies a shipping exception in step one, but when the refund API returns an unexpected error in step two, the agent silently skips the refund and reports the case as resolved. No single-turn accuracy test would catch that failure. For this reason, AI agents must be evaluated on behavioral dimensions, consistency, safety, resilience, and effectiveness across real-world conditions, not just on the text they generate.

This gap between how agents actually fail and what traditional metrics can detect points to a clear need: We need evaluation methods and frameworks that can capture how agents behave, not just one that simply checks the text they generate, things like success rates, reasoning quality, resilience to unexpected inputs, and how safely they can handle sensitive or risky situations.

The tooling ecosystem for agent evaluation is maturing rapidly. [MLflow](https://mlflow.org/) (v3.0+) now supports experiment tracing and built-in LLM judge capabilities. [TruLens](https://www.trulens.org/) enables pluggable feedback functions with OpenTelemetry integration. [LangChain Evals](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/) provides utilities for designing task-specific evaluation chains. [OpenAI Evals](https://github.com/openai/evals) offers a framework for model-graded metrics and version comparison. Finally, [Ragas](https://docs.ragas.io/) focuses on scoring the quality of retrieval-augmented responses. Feature sets evolve quickly across these tools, so it's worth checking each project's current documentation for precise capability boundaries. These and other emerging frameworks are making agent evaluation more structured and reproducible.

To make these ideas concrete, the rest of this article focuses on practical evaluation approaches you can apply - especially LLM-as-a-judge scoring, trace-based analysis, and repeatable test harnesses for multi-step agent workflows. The following code example demonstrates a minimal LLM-as-a-judge pattern using Claude and LangChain. The code evaluates a single-turn response for helpfulness and correctness, but the same approach extends naturally to multi-step agent traces, scoring tool-call sequences, retry behavior, and memory consistency across turns. This is a starter pattern, not a comprehensive benchmark taxonomy; adapt it to your own agent architecture, tooling, and evaluation needs.

```python
# Minimal, one-sample evaluation with Claude + LangChain
# Goal: show BOTH reference-free (helpfulness) and reference-aware (correctness) scoring.
# some of the finer points are  left for further exploration intentionally, to avoid excessive verbosity

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain.evaluation import load_evaluator

#  Pick a stable, versioned Claude model
# We use Sonnet 4.5 here; substitute any supported Claude model 
# (e.g., claude-haiku-4-5-20251001) depending on your access tier and cost/quality trade-off.
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929", temperature=0)
```
The following repository includes an end-to-end, runnable blueprint that demonstrates these patterns in code. You can use Claude and Langchain.

## Background

In a modern e-commerce environment, many critical workflows continue to rely heavily on human input: strategy development, data management, operational triage, issue resolution, and more. These workflows span ordering, product management, pricing, and payment instrument management. Over recent quarters, teams have begun developing and piloting AI agents to automate specific operations workflows: order exception triage, pricing and promotion validation, product catalog enrichment and policy checks, payment and refund issue investigation, and L2/L3 incident response across distributed commerce services.

These agents are typically evaluated first in controlled environments (e.g., sandbox APIs, replayed tickets, and synthetic edge cases) before being considered for production use. # Evaluating AI Agents in Practice: Benchmarks, Frameworks, and Lessons Learned

Mar 16, 2026 23
 min read

by

- [](/profile/Amit-Kumar-Padhy/) [Amit Kumar Padhy](/profile/Amit-Kumar-Padhy/)

reviewed by

- [](/profile/Arthur-Casals/) [Arthur Casals](/profile/Arthur-Casals/)

#### Write for InfoQ

**Feed your curiosity.** Help 550k+ global  
senior developers  
each month stay ahead.[Get in touch](https://www.infoq.com/write-for-infoq/)

 Listen to this article - 0:00

Audio ready to play

0:00

1.25x

1.5x

Like

- [Reading list](/showbookmarks.action)

### Key Takeaways

- Agents are systems not models – evaluate them accordingly. AI agents plan, call tools, maintain state, and adapt across multiple turns. Single-turn accuracy metrics and classical natural language processing (NLP) benchmarks like bilingual evaluation understudy (BLEU) and recall-oriented understudy for gisting evaluation (ROUGE) don't capture how agents fail in practice. Evaluation must target the full system's behavior over time.
- Behavior beats benchmarks. Task success, graceful recovery from tool failures, and consistency under real-world variability matter more than scoring well on curated test sets. An agent that works perfectly in a sandbox but silently misreports a failed refund in production hasn't passed any evaluation that counts.
- Hybrid evaluation is non-negotiable. Automated scoring (LLM-as-a-judge, trace analysis, and load testing) gives you repeatability and scale. Human judgment captures what automation misses: tone, trust, and contextual appropriateness. The best evaluation pipelines combine both, continuously.
- Operational constraints are first-class evaluation targets. Latency, cost per task, token efficiency, tool reliability, and policy compliance aren't afterthoughts, they are what determines whether a technically capable agent is viable at enterprise scale.
- Safety, governance, and user trust complete the picture. Red teaming, PII handling, permission boundary testing, and user experience scoring are as critical as accuracy. A technically brilliant agent that violates privacy boundaries or confuses users is a liability, not an asset.

## Introduction

You may have seen teams in your organization leveraging AI agents for demos, experiments, testing workflows where everything works perfectly. The agent plans, reasons, picks the right tool, and executes flawlessly during experiments. In production, the system fails or exhibits suboptimal behavior, and no one is quite sure whether the "smart" agent is actually reliable.

This article is for engineering and ML teams moving tool-using AI agents from prototype to production. It offers a practical evaluation framework, covering what to measure, how to measure it, and which tools to use, so you can catch failures before your users do.

The examples and code snippets in this article are intentionally minimal and illustrative, a one-sample Claude + LangChain evaluation meant to demonstrate both reference-free (helpfulness) and reference-aware (correctness) scoring, with a stable, versioned model for reproducibility. Production-grade evaluation pipelines require additional hardening around reliability, governance, cost control, version management, and data protection. In production setups, it is a good practice to use a separate judge model to reduce self-grading bias as shown in the code example.

#### Related Sponsors

- ##### [The missing layer in the agentic AI stack: Why AI applications need durable sessions](/url/f/3281e7f2-0151-48c1-8758-58e8bada3605/)

#### Related Sponsor

[![Related sponsor icon](https://imgopt.infoq.com//fit-in/290x500/filters:quality(100)/filters:no_upscale()/sponsorship/topic/ad80a710-b4db-4a6e-a702-28ca161a5276/AblyLogoMicrosite-1774947433903.jpg)](/url/f/57a18054-6981-4c3c-88ab-9fc6d642ae71/)

**Drop in Ably AI Transport.**  
Purpose-built infrastructure for the entire agent-to-user experience. **[Start building](/url/f/9e946c82-c425-4f68-b481-2a121e5f76dd/).**

In traditional software engineering, systems are rigorously tested before being deployed to production. AI agents, however, challenge this practice. While teams often validate individual models using established benchmarks, these evaluations rarely extend to the full agentic system operating in realistic environments. Unlike standard LLMs that generate single-turn text responses, AI agents are composite systems: They plan actions, invoke tools and APIs, maintain memory across interactions, and adapt their behavior over multiple steps and sessions. Classical NLP metrics like BLEU or ROUGE weren't designed for this situation; they score static text, not dynamic behavior. Consider a concrete example: An order-triage agent correctly identifies a shipping exception in step one, but when the refund API returns an unexpected error in step two, the agent silently skips the refund and reports the case as resolved. No single-turn accuracy test would catch that failure. For this reason, AI agents must be evaluated on behavioral dimensions, consistency, safety, resilience, and effectiveness across real-world conditions, not just on the text they generate.

This gap between how agents actually fail and what traditional metrics can detect points to a clear need: We need evaluation methods and frameworks that can capture how agents behave, not just one that simply checks the text they generate, things like success rates, reasoning quality, resilience to unexpected inputs, and how safely they can handle sensitive or risky situations.

The tooling ecosystem for agent evaluation is maturing rapidly. [MLflow](https://mlflow.org/) (v3.0+) now supports experiment tracing and built-in LLM judge capabilities. [TruLens](https://www.trulens.org/) enables pluggable feedback functions with OpenTelemetry integration. [LangChain Evals](https://docs.smith.langchain.com/) provides utilities for designing task-specific evaluation chains. [OpenAI Evals](https://github.com/openai/evals) offers a framework for model-graded metrics and version comparison. Finally, [Ragas](https://docs.ragas.io/) focuses on scoring the quality of retrieval-augmented responses. Feature sets evolve quickly across these tools, so it's worth checking each project's current documentation for precise capability boundaries. These and other emerging frameworks are making agent evaluation more structured and reproducible.

To make these ideas concrete, the rest of this article focuses on practical evaluation approaches you can apply - especially LLM-as-a-judge scoring, trace-based analysis, and repeatable test harnesses for multi-step agent workflows. The following code example demonstrates a minimal LLM-as-a-judge pattern using Claude and LangChain. The code evaluates a single-turn response for helpfulness and correctness, but the same approach extends naturally to multi-step agent traces, scoring tool-call sequences, retry behavior, and memory consistency across turns. This is a starter pattern, not a comprehensive benchmark taxonomy; adapt it to your own agent architecture, tooling, and evaluation needs.

```python
# Minimal, one-sample evaluation with Claude + LangChain
# Goal: show BOTH reference-free (helpfulness) and reference-aware (correctness) scoring.
# some of the finer points are  left for further exploration intentionally, to avoid excessive verbosity

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain.evaluation import load_evaluator

#  Pick a stable, versioned Claude model
# We use Sonnet 4.5 here; substitute any supported Claude model 
# (e.g., claude-haiku-4-5-20251001) depending on your access tier and cost/quality trade-off.
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929", temperature=0)
```
The following repository includes an end-to-end, runnable blueprint that demonstrates these patterns in code. You can use Claude and Langchain.

## Background

In a modern e-commerce environment, many critical workflows continue to rely heavily on human input: strategy development, data management, operational triage, issue resolution, and more. These workflows span ordering, product management, pricing, and payment instrument management. Over recent quarters, teams have begun developing and piloting AI agents to automate specific operations workflows: order exception triage, pricing and promotion validation, product catalog enrichment and policy checks, payment and refund issue investigation, and L2/L3 incident response across distributed commerce services.

These agents are typically evaluated first in controlled environments (e.g., sandbox APIs, replayed tickets, and synthetic edge cases) before being considered for production use. Goal: show BOTH reference-free (helpfulness) and reference-aware (correctness) scoring.
1) Pick a stable, versioned Claude model (use your tenant's ID/alias if different).
2) One gold sample keeps the snippet readable for the paper.

---

# Agent Skill Evaluation Methodology: Deep Research Report

**Date:** 2026-06-23 | **Sources:** 7 primary sources, ~80k tokens of content analyzed

---

## Executive Summary

Agent skill evaluation is a rapidly maturing discipline that differs fundamentally from traditional LLM evaluation. Because agents execute multi-turn, tool-using, state-modifying workflows, evaluation must target **behavioral trajectories** — not just text outputs. The field has converged around a hybrid methodology combining deterministic code graders, model-based rubric scoring, and human calibration, applied across four dimensions: task completion, output quality, safety/constraints, and process correctness.

---

## 1. Core Framework: What to Evaluate

### The Four Evaluation Dimensions

The most consistent framework across sources defines four orthogonal dimensions ([Anthropic, Jan 2026](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); [Harness Engineering Academy, Mar 2026](https://harnessengineering.academy/blog/building-evaluation-datasets-for-ai-agents-a-step-by-step-guide/)):

| Dimension | What It Measures | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Task completion** | Did the agent accomplish the user's goal? | Code runs, reservation booked, answer returned |
| **Output quality** | Is the result accurate, complete, coherent? | Summary covers key points, code follows conventions |
| **Safety & constraints** | Did the agent stay within boundaries? | No hallucinated facts, no scope creep, within cost budget |
| **Process correctness** | Was the trajectory reasonable? | Correct tool selection, no unnecessary steps, proper ordering |

### OpenAI's Four-Category Model (Skill-Specific)

For **skill evaluation specifically**, OpenAI frames success across ([OpenAI Developers Blog, Jun 2026](https://developers.openai.com/blog/eval-skills)):

- **Outcome goals:** Did the task complete? Does the app run?
- **Process goals:** Did the agent invoke the skill and follow intended tools/steps?
- **Style goals:** Does output follow desired conventions?
- **Efficiency goals:** Did it get there without thrashing (unnecessary commands, excessive tokens)?

**Key insight:** "Define success *before* you write the skill. A useful way to think about this is to split your checks into categories." Skills with vague or overloaded descriptions fail to trigger reliably — the name and description are the primary routing signals.

---

## 2. The Three-Type Grader Architecture

All major sources converge on a **three-tier grader system** ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); [Harness Engineering](https://harnessengineering.academy/blog/building-evaluation-datasets-for-ai-agents-a-step-by-step-guide/); [InfoQ, Mar 2026](https://www.infoq.com/articles/evaluating-ai-agents-lessons-learned/)):

### Tier 1: Deterministic Code Graders
- String/regex matching, file existence checks, command trace analysis
- Static analysis (linting, type checking, security scanning)
- Tool-call verification (was the right tool called, with correct parameters?)
- **Strengths:** Fast, cheap, objective, reproducible
- **Weakness:** Brittle to valid variations; no nuance

### Tier 2: Model-Based (LLM-as-Judge) Graders
- Rubric-based scoring with structured schemas
- Natural language assertions against criteria
- Pairwise comparison and reference-based evaluation
- Multi-judge consensus (3-vote adversarial verification)
- **Strengths:** Flexible, scalable, captures nuance
- **Weakness:** Non-deterministic; requires calibration against human judgment

### Tier 3: Human Graders
- SME review, spot-check sampling, inter-annotator agreement
- Used to calibrate model-based graders, not as primary evaluator
- **Strengths:** Gold standard; catches what automation misses
- **Weakness:** Expensive, slow, hard to scale

**Production cascade pattern:** Deterministic checks run first (instant, free) → model-based grading runs on passing examples (seconds, cents) → human evaluation runs on a calibration sample (minutes, dollars).

---

## 3. Skill-Specific Evaluation: Triggering, Execution, and Quality

### The Three-Phase Evaluation for Skills

For agent skills specifically (SKILL.md-based systems), evaluation must cover three distinct phases:

1. **Triggering evaluation** — Does the skill activate when it should?
   - Test explicit invocation (user names the skill directly)
   - Test implicit invocation (user describes the scenario without naming the skill)
   - Test negative controls (adjacent requests that should *not* trigger the skill)
   - OpenAI recommends a small CSV of 10-20 prompts with `should_trigger` true/false labels

2. **Execution evaluation** — Did the agent follow the skill's intended steps?
   - Parse JSONL traces for command execution events
   - Verify tool calls against expected sequences
   - Check for skipped steps, out-of-order execution, or missing dependencies

3. **Output quality evaluation** — Does the result meet the skill's definition of done?
   - File existence checks (deterministic)
   - Code quality rubrics (model-based with structured schema)
   - Convention compliance (style, naming, structure)

### Progressive Disclosure and Token Efficiency

Agent skills use a three-phase loading architecture ([Medium/Spillwave, Jan 2026](https://medium.com/spillwave-solutions/agent-skills-the-universal-standard-transforming-how-ai-agents-work-fc7397406e2e)):

| Phase | What Loads | Tokens per Skill |
|---|---|---|
| **Discovery** | YAML frontmatter (name + description) | ~100 |
| **Activation** | Full SKILL.md instructions | <5,000 |
| **Execution** | Scripts, references, assets | As needed |

This uses **96% fewer tokens** than loading all skill instructions upfront. Evaluation must therefore also test whether the skill's description is precise enough for correct routing without reading full instructions.

---

## 4. Building Eval Datasets

### Dataset Design Principles

1. **Seed from real interactions, not imagination.** Synthetic examples reflect your assumptions; real examples reflect what users actually do. Start with production logs, focusing on clear successes, clear failures, and ambiguous outcomes (the most valuable category).

2. **Collect full traces, not just input/output.** For each example, store: user input, final output, every intermediate step (tool calls, reasoning), available context, and metadata (latency, token count, cost).

3. **Build systematic coverage** across:
   - **Input types** (weighted by production frequency)
   - **Difficulty** (40% easy, 40% medium, 20% hard)
   - **Failure modes** (hallucination, wrong tool selection, infinite loops, cost overruns, constraint violations, partial completion)
   - **Context variation** (different available tools, missing info, contradictory context)

4. **Target 200-500 examples** for initial coverage. Start with 50 seed examples, then add 20-30 per week as new failure modes emerge.

### Synthetic Data Generation

Databricks' approach ([Databricks Blog, Dec 2024](https://www.databricks.com/blog/streamline-ai-agent-evaluation-with-new-synthetic-data-capabilities)) generates eval sets from proprietary data using an LLM:
- Input: documents + number of questions + optional guidelines
- Output: `<question, synthetic answer, source document>` tuples
- SMEs review and edit factual answers (faster than reviewing full responses)
- Customer reported **60% relative quality improvement** before SME involvement

---

## 5. Capability vs. Regression Evals

A critical distinction ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)):

| Type | Question | Pass Rate Target | Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capability** | "What can this agent do?" | Start low, climb | R&D phase; add harder tasks over time |
| **Regression** | "Does it still do what it used to?" | ~100% | Post-launch; protect against backsliding |

**Graduation pattern:** When a capability eval reaches high pass rates, it "graduates" to become a regression test — shifting from "Can we do this at all?" to "Can we still do this reliably?"

---

## 6. Handling Non-Determinism

Agent evaluations must account for stochastic outputs:

- **Run multiple trials per task** to produce consistent aggregate scores
- **Use a separate judge model** to reduce self-grading bias (e.g., evaluate a GPT-built skill with Claude as judge)
- **Adversarial verification:** 3 independent judges per claim; kill if 2/3 refute (prevents plausible-but-wrong findings from surviving)
- **Score calibration:** Periodic human spot-checks to verify model-based graders still agree with human judgment

---

## 7. Operational Constraints as First-Class Targets

Per InfoQ's synthesis, these are **not afterthoughts** — they determine viability at enterprise scale:

- **Latency** (time to first token, time to last token)
- **Cost per task** (total tokens, API calls)
- **Token efficiency** (wasted context, unnecessary reasoning)
- **Tool reliability** (failure rates for external calls)
- **Policy compliance** (permission boundaries, PII handling)

---

## 8. Ecosystem and Tooling (as of mid-2026)

| Tool | Focus | Key Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **DeepEval** | General LLM eval | LLM-as-judge, hallucination detection, prompt injection checks |
| **MLflow 3.0+** | Experiment tracing | Built-in LLM judge, integrated with Databricks |
| **LangChain Evals** | Task-specific chains | Reference-free + reference-aware scoring |
| **OpenAI Evals** | Codex skill testing | Deterministic graders + rubric-based grading via `--output-schema` |
| **TruLens** | Pluggable feedback | OpenTelemetry integration, feedback functions |
| **Ragas** | RAG evaluation | Retrieval-augmented response quality scoring |
| **SkillsMP** | Skill marketplace | Indexes 1.8M+ SKILL.md files, occupation-based taxonomy |

---

## 9. Key Takeaways and Methodological Principles

1. **Evals force you to specify what success means.** The act of writing evals is as valuable as running them — it resolves ambiguity between team members about expected behavior.

2. **Start small, grow iteratively.** 10-20 targeted prompts with deterministic graders beats a massive benchmark you can't maintain. Add model-based graders once deterministic checks are solid.

3. **Trajectory matters more than output.** An agent that produces the right answer through lucky guesses will fail on the next input. Evaluate the path, not just the destination.

4. **Negative controls are essential.** Testing that a skill does NOT trigger when it shouldn't catches false positives — often more dangerous than false negatives.

5. **Dataset drift is as real as model drift.** Version your eval datasets, tag examples by date, audit monthly, and retire stale examples.

6. **Hybrid grading is non-negotiable.** Automated scoring gives repeatability and scale; human judgment captures tone, trust, and contextual appropriateness. The best pipelines combine both continuously.

---

# Agent Skill Evaluation Methodology: Deep Research Report

**Date:** 2026-06-23 | **Sources:** 7 primary sources, ~80k tokens of content analyzed

---

## Executive Summary

Agent skill evaluation is a rapidly maturing discipline that differs fundamentally from traditional LLM evaluation. Because agents execute multi-turn, tool-using, state-modifying workflows, evaluation must target **behavioral trajectories** — not just text outputs. The field has converged around a hybrid methodology combining deterministic code graders, model-based rubric scoring, and human calibration, applied across four dimensions: task completion, output quality, safety/constraints, and process correctness.

---

## 1. Core Framework: What to Evaluate

### The Four Evaluation Dimensions

The most consistent framework across sources defines four orthogonal dimensions ([Anthropic, Jan 2026](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); [Harness Engineering Academy, Mar 2026](https://harnessengineering.academy/blog/building-evaluation-datasets-for-ai-agents-a-step-by-step-guide/)):

| Dimension | What It Measures | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Task completion** | Did the agent accomplish the user's goal? | Code runs, reservation booked, answer returned |
| **Output quality** | Is the result accurate, complete, coherent? | Summary covers key points, code follows conventions |
| **Safety & constraints** | Did the agent stay within boundaries? | No hallucinated facts, no scope creep, within cost budget |
| **Process correctness** | Was the trajectory reasonable? | Correct tool selection, no unnecessary steps, proper ordering |

### OpenAI's Four-Category Model (Skill-Specific)

For **skill evaluation specifically**, OpenAI frames success across ([OpenAI Developers Blog, Jun 2026](https://developers.openai.com/blog/eval-skills)):

- **Outcome goals:** Did the task complete? Does the app run?
- **Process goals:** Did the agent invoke the skill and follow intended tools/steps?
- **Style goals:** Does output follow desired conventions?
- **Efficiency goals:** Did it get there without thrashing (unnecessary commands, excessive tokens)?

**Key insight:** "Define success *before* you write the skill. A useful way to think about this is to split your checks into categories." Skills with vague or overloaded descriptions fail to trigger reliably — the name and description are the primary routing signals.

---

## 2. The Three-Type Grader Architecture

All major sources converge on a **three-tier grader system** ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); [Harness Engineering](https://harnessengineering.academy/blog/building-evaluation-datasets-for-ai-agents-a-step-by-step-guide/); [InfoQ, Mar 2026](https://www.infoq.com/articles/evaluating-ai-agents-lessons-learned/)):

### Tier 1: Deterministic Code Graders
- String/regex matching, file existence checks, command trace analysis
- Static analysis (linting, type checking, security scanning)
- Tool-call verification (was the right tool called, with correct parameters?)
- **Strengths:** Fast, cheap, objective, reproducible
- **Weakness:** Brittle to valid variations; no nuance

### Tier 2: Model-Based (LLM-as-Judge) Graders
- Rubric-based scoring with structured schemas
- Natural language assertions against criteria
- Pairwise comparison and reference-based evaluation
- Multi-judge consensus (3-vote adversarial verification)
- **Strengths:** Flexible, scalable, captures nuance
- **Weakness:** Non-deterministic; requires calibration against human judgment

### Tier 3: Human Graders
- SME review, spot-check sampling, inter-annotator agreement
- Used to calibrate model-based graders, not as primary evaluator
- **Strengths:** Gold standard; catches what automation misses
- **Weakness:** Expensive, slow, hard to scale

**Production cascade pattern:** Deterministic checks run first (instant, free) → model-based grading runs on passing examples (seconds, cents) → human evaluation runs on a calibration sample (minutes, dollars).
